coredata.dc:title: Near-infrared light photocatalysis and photothermy of carbon quantum dots and au nanoparticles loaded titania nanotube array

coredata.dc:creator: Jin, Chen
Su, Kun
Tan, Lei
Liu, Xiangmei
Cui, Zhenduo
Yang, Xianjin
Li, Zhaoyang
Liang, Yanqin
Zhu, Shengli
Yeung, Kelvin Wai Kwok
Wu, Shuilin
coredata.dc:description: The excellent photocatalysis is quite important for coating materials on implants, which can be photoinspired to kill bacteria by locally producing the reactive oxygen species (ROS) within a very short time. Herein, we developed a novel photocatalytic system of Au nanoparticles and carbon quantum dots loaded TiO2 nanotube array (TNTs/Au/CDs) on the surface of titanium. This system can exhibit enhanced near-infrared (NIR) light photocatalysis and photothermy because CDs can convert NIR light into the light of 500–600 nm and transfer electrons, which can inspire nano Au-catalyzed TNTs to generate ROS and enhance the surface plasmon resonance (SPR) effect of Au nanoparticles to produce more local photothermy. The enhanced photocatalysis and local photothermy can exhibit a synergistic effect to kill the bacteria highly effectively within a very short time by damaging the membranes of the bacteria. The antibacterial efficiency of the modified surface is 96.19% and 99.89% against Staphylococcus aureus and Escherichia coli, respectively, under 808 nm NIR light irradiation for 15 min.
coredata.dcterms:subject: NIR photocatalysis
Antibacterial
Au nanoparticles
Upconversion
Carbon quantum dots

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Preparation of Au NPs and CDs
Preparation of TNTs/CDs, TNTs/Au and TNTs/Au/CDs
Photocatalytic test under 808 nm NIR light irradiation
In vitro antibacterial tests under 808 nm NIR light irradiation
Cell viability and osteogenic differentiation assay
Mechanism for the highly improved photocatalytic performance of TNTs/Au/CDs
Photocatalytic and photothermal disinfection under 808 nm light irradiation

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: SEM image of a) TNTs and b) TNTs/Au/CDs. c) EDS tests of TNTs and TNTs/Au/CDs (scale bar is 100 nm). The section image of d) TNTs/Au/CDs in low-resolution and e) TNTs/Au/CDs in high-resolution (scale bar is 100 nm). f) Corresponding EDS test of the TNTs/Au/CDs in red flame in panel e.
The TEM image of the a) TNTs, b) TNTs/Au/CDs (scale bar is 100 nm), c) the HRTEM image of the TNTs/Au/CDs (scale bar is 10 nm), and d) the SEAD pattern of the TNTs/Au/CDs.

a) The UV–visible absorption spectrum of samples. b) The upconversion spectrum of CDs. c) The photoluminescence of samples. d) The transient photocurrent responses of samples. e) The EIS of samples. f) The ROS detection of samples under the 808 nm NIR light irradiation. g) The mechanism of the enhanced photocatalysis and photothermal effect by 808 nm NIR light irradiation.

a) The photothermal curves of Ti, TNTs, TNTs/CDs, TNTs/Au and TNTs/Au/CDs under the 808 nm NIR light irradiation. b) Photographs and infrared thermal images of the samples in 96-wells with 200 μL PBS after 808 nm NIR irradiation.

The antibacterial assay in vitro. The antibacterial efficiency against a) S. aureus and b) E. coli under 15 min 808 nm NIR light irradiation; the fluorescent-based cell live/dead assay of, c) S. aureus, and d) E. coli (scale bar is 50 μm). (n = 3, mean ± SD.).

The SEM image of a) S. aureus and b) E. coli after 15 min 808 nm NIR light irradiation (scale bar is 500 nm). The protein leakage (determined at OD 562 nm) from c) S. aureus, and d) E. coli after 15 min 808 nm NIR light irradiation. The ATP level of, e) S. aureus, and f) E. coli after 15 min 808 nm NIR light irradiation. (n = 3, mean ± SD.).
The schematical illustration of antibacterial mechanism of TNTs/Au/CDs under 808 nm NIR light irradiation within 15 min.

The cytotoxicity and ALP activity evaluation in vitro. a) The cell fluorescence staining of samples after culturing for 24 h (scale bar is 50 μm). b) The cell viability of the samples at 1, 3, 7 days. c) The ALP activity evaluation of samples at 3, 7, 14. (n = 3, mean ± SD.).
Schematical illustration of the synthetic route of TNTs/Au/CDs.

head.ce:title: Near-infrared light photocatalysis and photothermy of carbon quantum dots and au nanoparticles loaded titania nanotube array

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: The Key Laboratory of Advanced Ceramics and Machining Technology by the Ministry of Education of China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The excellent photocatalysis is quite important for coating materials on implants, which can be photoinspired to kill bacteria by locally producing the reactive oxygen species (ROS) within a very short time. Herein, we developed a novel photocatalytic system of Au nanoparticles and carbon quantum dots loaded TiO2 nanotube array (TNTs/Au/CDs) on the surface of titanium. This system can exhibit enhanced near-infrared (NIR) light photocatalysis and photothermy because CDs can convert NIR light into the light of 500–600 nm and transfer electrons, which can inspire nano Au-catalyzed TNTs to generate ROS and enhance the surface plasmon resonance (SPR) effect of Au nanoparticles to produce more local photothermy. The enhanced photocatalysis and local photothermy can exhibit a synergistic effect to kill the bacteria highly effectively within a very short time by damaging the membranes of the bacteria. The antibacterial efficiency of the modified surface is 96.19% and 99.89% against Staphylococcus aureus and Escherichia coli, respectively, under 808 nm NIR light irradiation for 15 min.

ce:section.ce:para: Currently, many great efforts have been made for preventing implant-related bacterial infection [1]. On the one hand, the therapy for bacterial infections often bring to the patients more pain, there are about 13 million deaths all over the world every year due to the bacterial infection and this number is increasing rapidly [2]. On the other hand, it is difficult to cure once biofilm forms on the surface of implants by antibiotics clinically [3]. The commonly used methods for killing bacteria include inorganic antibacterial agents [4]. metallic antibacterial agents [5], organic antibacterial agents [6] and antibiotic [7]. All currently used strategies exhibit some shortcomings such as cytotoxicity and tissue toxicity. In addition, the abuse of antibiotics can cause the tolerance of bacteria [8,9]. Besides antibiotics, it has been reported that the long-term use of metallic antibacterial agent like Ag nanoparticles can also induce bacterial resistance [10]. Therefore, it is necessary to find exogenous antibacterial strategies without antibiotics.

In recent year, those materials with excellent photocatalytic properties can be the promising candidates for photodynamic therapy (PDT) against bacterial infections because these materials can produce radical oxygen species (ROS) under light irradiation to kill bacteria [11]. However, the widely used photocatalytic materials such as TiO2 and ZnO have a wide energy gap, which can only be stimulated under the ultraviolet light and visible light [12,13], but the ultraviolet light and visible light have poor penetrability, which cannot penetrate tissues. Different from the ultraviolet light and visible light, the near-infrared (NIR) light has excellent penetrability [14,15] but it cannot stimulate most photocatalytic materials. To solve these problems, the upconversion nanoparticles (UCNPs) such as carbon quantum dots (CDs) [16] have attracted much attention in the world. Because of the nanometer scale, CDs have unique characteristics of up-conversion photoluminescence (UCPL), which can transform NIR light to the ultraviolet light and visible light [17,18], indicating that CDs can make NIR light to stimulate the photocatalytic materials.

Unfortunately, on the one hand, rapid recombination of electrons and holes is the main limit of photocatalytic materials. On the other hand, despite the superior penetrability, the energy of NIR light decreases as the penetration deepens. These deficiencies will weaken the photocatalytic effect and ROS yields of the photocatalytic materials. Therefore, some measurements have been employed to overcome the deficiencies of photocatalytic materials, such as combining with metal nanoparticles [19,20], or establishing the heterostructures [21,22]. Au nanoparticles (Au NPs) are widely used in photocatalysis domain because of the surface plasmon resonance (SPR) effect [23] The decoration of Au NPs on TiO2 can form the Schottky barriers, which can prevent the recombination of electrons and holes and enhance the photocatalytic performance of the decorated TiO2 [24]. In addition, because of the shaking of the surface electrons on the Au NPs after NIR light irradiation, the photothermal effect occurs [25,26], so the Au NPs decoration can enhance the photocatalytic activity and photothermal performances of TiO2.

To solve the problem of rapid recombination of electrons and holes and no NIR light corresponding for TiO2, in this work, the unique ternary composite of TNTs loaded with the Au NPs and CDs (TNTs/Au/CDs) has been synthesized via a facile way (Scheme 1 ). The upconversion property of CDs and the catalysis of Au NPs enhance the photocatalytic property of the TNTs to produce ROS under 808 nm NIR light irradiation while Au NPs can produce hyperthermia under NIR irradiation. This ternary surface system has the excellent photocatalytic and photothermal performance, which endows the metallic implants with superior antibacterial efficacy within 15 min through the synergistic effect of ROS and local heat. Due to its superior antibacterial efficacy and osteogenesis performance, this system will have excellent application prospect in Bone implant biomaterial.

It is a typical synthesis of TNTs by anodization process. The pure Ti plate (99.6%, Φ6 mm × 2.5 mm, Baosteel Group Corp, Shanghai, China) was used as base materials. These plates were polished by SiC sandpapers with different grits and rinsed with acetone, ethanol and deionized (DI) water. These polished Ti plates were treated with etching liquid, which was composed with HNO3, HF and H2O. The anodization (Ti plate is anode, the graphite is cathode) was carried out in the electrolyte composed of NH4F and ethylene glycol solution at room temperature for 4 h with a voltage of 40 V. The TNTs were then calcined at 450 °C for 2 h.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of Au NPs and CDs
ce:section.ce:para: 100 mL 0.01% (wt%) HAuCl4 was added into the flask with a reflux condenser and kept stirring. The 3 mL solution of 3% trisodium citrate was added into the flask at 100 °C with continuous stirring for 20 min until the color of the solution turned into the deep red. When the temperature was back to the room temperature, the 400 mg polyvinylpyrrolidone (PVP, Mw = 55,000) was added into the flask with stirring for 24 h. The stabilized Au NPs were obtained by centrifuging at 13,000 rpm for 30 min and washed 3 times by DI water, and then were dispersed in 8 mL DI water, the amount of Au NPs (wt%) is 0.125%.

To prepare the CDs, 2 g ethylenediamine and 2 g citric acid were dissolved in 20 mL ethanol in a Teflon equipped stainless steel autoclave (100 mL) at 180 °C for 3 h. At the end of the reaction, the clay bank solution was obtained and centrifuged at 3000 rpm for 15 min to get rid of the bigger particles, and then dialyzed against water (MW cutoff 1000 Da) for 2 days to remove the smaller impurities to get the CDs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of TNTs/CDs, TNTs/Au and TNTs/Au/CDs
ce:section.ce:para: The TNTs were divided into 3 groups. These 3 groups were separately dipped in the solutions of CDs, Au NPs and Au NPs/CDs (volume ratio of Au NPs:CDs is 1:2) in a vacuum environment for 24 h, then the 3 groups were dried at 50 °C for 12 h to obtain the samples of TNTs/CDs, TNTs/Au and TNTs/Au/CDs.

To obtain the surface morphology and internal structure image of the samples, the scanning electron microscopy (SEM, SM-6510LV, JEOL, Japan) and transmission electron microscopy and high-resolution transmission electron microscopy (TEM and HRTEM, Tecnai G20 electron microscope, FEI, USA) were used. The content of chemical compositions of the samples was analysed with the Energy Dispersive Spectroscopy (EDS) and X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS, ESCALAB 250Xi, Thermo Scientific, USA). The crystallinity of the samples was determined by the X-ray powder diffractometer (XRD, D8A25, Bruker, Germany) with Cu Kα radiation (λ = 1.54051 and 1.54433 Å).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Photocatalytic test under 808 nm NIR light irradiation
ce:section.ce:para: The spectrophotometer (UV-3600, Japan) was used to get the UV–visible absorption spectra of the samples. The upconversion fluorescence was tested to get the fluorescence of the CDs excited with light of 808 nm by the fluorospectrophotometer (F-280, China). To analyze the lifetime of the photoelectron, the photoluminescence (PL) was tested by the fluorospectrophotometer (LS-55, PE, USA). The photocurrent measurements of the Ti, TNTs, TNTs/CDs, TNTs/Au and TNTs/CDs/Au were carried out by an electrochemical workstation (CHI660E), the 808 nm laser machine is made by Hi-Tech Optoelectronics Co., Ltd. The intensity of NIR is 0.6 W/cm2, the exposed illuminated area is 2.5447 cm2. The samples are the working electrode, the Pt is the counter electrode and the Ag/AgCl is the reference electrode, the electrolyte is 0.5 M Na2SO4. The photocurrent and electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) were tested in −0.5 V in an electrochemical workstation.

To detect the ROS yields of the samples, the 2′,7′-dichlorodi-hydrofluorescein diacetate (DCFH) was used to react with ROS to produce the DCF. The samples were immersed into 200 μL DCFH solution in the 96 well plates. The examination was taken every 2 min (15 min) to get the fluorescence of the DCF solution after 808 nm NIR irradiation.

The five groups (Ti, TNTs, TNTs/CDs. TNTs/Au and TNTs/Au/CDs) were placed into 96-wells plates with 150 μL PBS in each well under 808 nm NIR light irradiation for 15 min. The infrared thermal images were obtained by infrared imaging device (E50) every 2 min.

ce:section.ce:section-title: In vitro antibacterial tests under 808 nm NIR light irradiation
ce:section.ce:para:
Staphylococcus aureus (S. aureus, ATCC 25923) and Escherichia coli (E. coli, ATCC 8099), the most common two types of pathogenic bacteria, were chosen to test the antibacterial activities of the samples. Firstly, the samples were placed in 96-well plates and the 150 μL 1 × 105 colony-forming units (CFU)/mL bacterial liquids were added in each well. The antibacterial efficacy of each group was measured under three kinds of conditions: after 15 min 808 nm NIR light irradiation, after culturing in the dark for 15 min and after culturing in the ice-bath for 15 min. After the treatment, the 10 μL bacterial liquid was extracted out and spread on the Luria-Bertain (LB) solid medium after dilution. The agar plates were cultured at 37 °C for 24 h. The antibacterial efficiency (AE) can be evaluated with counting the number of bacterial colonies on the plate according to the following formula (number of bacterial colonies in control group-Nc and number of bacterial colonies in experimental group-Ne):
(1)
AE =
Nc − Ne
/ Nc

After the antibacterial tests, the samples were taken out and the 2.5% glutaraldehyde was used to fix the bacteria onto the surface of the samples for 2 h, the series of ethanol solutions (30, 50, 70, 90 and 100% v/v) were used to dehydrate for 15 min each sequentially. Finally, the surface morphologies of bacteria were observed by SEM.

The status of the bacteria on the surface was observed by the fluorescent-based bacteria live/dead test. The above-mentioned bacterial liquids were added in the 96 wells. After 15 min 808 nm NIR light irradiation, the bacterial liquids were taken out and the bacteria on the samples were dyed with STYO9 and PI (LIVE/DEAD Baclight Bacterial Viability Kit, Beyotime) for 15 min in sequence and washed 3 times by PBS. Finally, the images (green fluorescence means the live bacteria and red fluorescence means the dead bacteria) were obtained by an inverted fluorescence microscope (IFM, Olympus, IX73).

In order to further investigate the antibacterial mechanism of the samples, the enhanced BCA Protein Assay Kit (cat# P0010, Beyotime, China) was used to detect the protein leakage of the bacteria. Firstly, Ti (control group), TNTs, TNTs/CDs, TNTs/Au and TNTs/Au/CDs were cultured with 200 μL both E. coli and S. aureus with 5 × 104 CFU/mL, each sample was divided into two groups (under 808 nm NIR irradiation for 15 min and in the dark for 15 min). Then these bacterial solutions were centrifuged with 10,000 rpm at 4 °C for 5 min. The 25 μL supernatant liquid was added into 200 μL BCA working liquid in 96 well plates and cultured in 37 °C for 30 min. Finally, the microplate reader was utilized to measure the protein leakage. The ATP is bacterial metabolism energy unit. The 200 μL 5 × 104 CFU/mL bacterial suspensions were added onto the samples, after treatments, the bacterial solution was centrifuged at 5000 rpm for 10 min at 4 °C, then 300 μL lysis buffer was added and followed by shocking with an ultrasonic cell disruptor (vcx500; Sonics) for 5 min in an ice bath. Finally, the solution was transferred into 96-well plates with ATP working solution and measured with the microplate reader (SpectraMax i3, Molecular Devices).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Cell viability and osteogenic differentiation assay
ce:section.ce:para: In order to test the cytocompatibility of the samples, the cells morphologies after culturing for 1 day were observed by fluorochrome (Yisheng, Shanghai). The cell nutrient solution was taken out and the cells were washed with PBS and stained with Fluorescein isothiocyanate (FITC) for 30 min in the dark, then washed 3 times with PBS. Then the cells were stained with 4′,6-diamidino-2-phenylindole (DAPI) for 30 s and washed with PBS for 3 times in darkness.

The 3-[4,5-dimethylthiazol-2-yl]-2,5-diphenyl tetrazolium bromide (MTT) assay was used to detect the cell viability of the samples for MC3T3-E1 cells (Tongji Hospital, Wuhan). The cells were cultured with the samples for 1, 3 and 7 days in an incubator at 37 °C with 5% CO2, at the setting time, the cell nutrient solution in each well was taken out and 200 μL 0.5 mg/mL MTT solution was added into each well, then shocked for another 4 h at 37 °C. Then the MTT solution in each well was taken out and 200 μL dimethyl sulfoxide (DMSO) was added with continuous shaking for 15 min. Finally, the absorbance of DMSO in 96-well plate was measured at 570 nm with a microplate reader. The alkaline phosphatase (ALP) test was made to investigate the osteogenic differentiation of cells. The MC3T3-E1 cells were seeded on the samples in 96-well plates, and the ALP test was performed at the setting time of 3, 7 and 14 days using the professional kit (Abcam, ab83369). After incubation, the 1% Triton X-100 solution was used to lyse the cells at 37 °C for 1 h, and then the ALP test was made with the professional ALP kit (Abcam, ab83369) using a microplate reader at 405 nm.

As shown in Fig. 1 , the nanotubes were successfully produced by an anodization process in Scheme 1. It is obviously that the four groups including TNTs (Fig. 1a), TNTs/CDs (Fig. S1a), TNTs/Au (Fig. S1b) and TNTs/Au/CDs (Fig. 1b) exhibit the same uniform tubular structure with the average diameter of about 90 nm. The difference is that there are some nanoparticles on the orifice of the tubes after loading CDs and Au NPs (marked with the red arrows in Figs. 1b, S1a and b). The EDS patterns show that compared with TNTs, there is a stronger C peak obtained from TNTs/CDs (Fig. S1b) and TNTs/Au/CDs (Fig. 1c), indicating that the CDs have been loaded in the TNTs. In addition, Au was detected from both TNTs/Au (Fig. S1d) and TNTs/Au/CDs (Fig. 1c), suggesting the successful loading of Au NPs in the TNTs. Fig. 1d is the cross-section image of the TNTs/Au/CDs, which shows the obvious interface between substrate and the TNTs, the thickness of the TNTs/Au/CDs is about 7 μm and the nanotubes are aligned well. The inside structure of the TNTs shown in Fig. 1e shows the nanoparticles in the inside of TNTs (marked by red frame). In addition, the corresponding EDS results shown in Fig. 1f exhibit much stronger signals of Au and C than those obtained from the surface (Fig. 1c), further indicating the successful loading of both Au NPs and CDs in the TNTs.

To further observe the inside structure of the samples, the TEM images of both TNTs and TNTs/Au/CDs are shown in Fig. 2 . The tubular structure can be observed in both Fig. 2a and b, and some nanoparticles are distributed in the nanotubes (Fig. 2b), the size range of nanoparticles is shown in Fig. S2, most nanoparticles have diameters of 9.8–15.5 nm. The high-resolution TEM (HRTEM) of the TNTs/Au/CDs in red flame in Fig. 2b is shown in Fig. 2c. The lattice fringe spacing of titania is 0.35 nm, which is assigned to the (101) plane of anatase TiO2 [27]. The nanoparticle with a diameter of about 15 nm has the lattice spacing of 0.23 nm, which corresponds to the typical (111) plane of the Au NPs [28]. The smaller nanoparticles with diameters less than 10 nm have a lattice spacing of 0.21 nm, which is the (100) peaks of the CDs [29]. In addition, the SAED pattern of the TNTs/Au/CDs is shown in Fig. 2d. Obviously, there exist (101), (111) and (100) peaks in the pattern, and the bright spots dropped on the ring diffraction pattern is due to the abundant edges of CDs and the existence of some nitrogen doping defects because ethane diamine was used as the raw material of the prepared CDs [30]. These above results indicate that the Au NPs and the CDs have been successfully loaded in the TNTs.

The XRD patterns of the TNTs, TNTs/CDs, TNTs/Au and TNTs/Au/CDs are shown in Fig. S3, the peaks at 25.5°, 37.8°, 47.8° and 54.0° are the typical peaks of anatase titania, and there is no significant lattice change after loading Au NPs and CDs because of less content of the Au NPs and CDs [31,32]. Fig. S4 shows the XPS spectra of the TNTs, TNTs/CDs, TNTs/Au and TNTs/Au/CDs. It is obvious that C signal can be detected from all the samples (Fig. S3a). The C signal obtained from TNTs and TNTs/Au should be come from the contamination in the air [33]. In contrast, the intensity of C signal in both TNTs/CDs and TNTs/Au/CDs becomes stronger than the two formers (Table S1), suggesting the successful loading of CDs. The XPS narrow spectra of Au4f (Fig. S3b) acquired from four kinds of samples disclose no signal of Au in TNTs and TNTs/CDs but weak signal in TNTs/Au and TNTs/Au/CDs (Table S1).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Mechanism for the highly improved photocatalytic performance of TNTs/Au/CDs
ce:section.ce:para: As shown in Fig. 3a, the UV–visible absorption spectra of the samples show that the pure Ti and TNTs have no obvious absorption in 200–1000 nm. In contrast, the TNTs/CDs and TNTs/Au show enhanced absorption in 300–900 nm due to the loading of CDs and Au NPs. The TNTs/Au/CDs exhibits the strongest absorption in 300–900 nm due to the synergistic effects of both CDs and AuNPs. Especially, the intensive absorption peak at 530 nm should be attributed to the SPR effect of Au NPs, which can also be strengthened by loaded CDs in TNTs. The upconverted property of the CDs was shown in Fig. 3b. When the 808 nm NIR light was used to excite the CDs, the strong emission peak around 500–650 nm proved that the CDs could upconvert the 808 nm NIR light into visible light. The photoluminescence (PL) measurement was performed to investigate the lifetime of the electron and hole pairs in the composites. As shown in Fig. 3c, the main peak at 484 nm is the characteristic peak of TiO2 [34], the other peaks are due to the band-edge free excitons. The weaker intensity of the spectrum indicates the longer lifetime of the photogenerated electrons, which suggests that the composites have the better photocatalytic activity. Since the Au NPs and the CDs can transfer electrons to the conduction band of TNTs, the lowest PL intensity of the TNTs/Au/CDs indicates that both Au NPs and CDs can enhance the photocatalytic performance of TNTs.

The photocurrent measurements shown in Fig. 3d disclose that the sample of TNTs/Au/CDs has the strongest photocurrent response from zero to 1.2 μA, which is much larger than the others. To further investigate the separation of electron and hole in the samples under 808 nm NIR light, the EIS spectra of samples were measured. The smaller arc size of EIS reflects the smaller limit for electrons transfer. As shown in Fig. 3e, the arc size of EIS of the TNTs/Au/CDs is minimum, indicating the most effective separation of electron and hole in TNTs/Au/CDs with 808 nm NIR light. In addition, 2′,7′-dichlorodihydrofluorescein diacetate (DCFH) was used to detect the ROS production of the samples under the 808 nm NIR. Since the fluorescence intensity of DCFH can be enhanced when it is oxidized by ROS. As shown in Fig. 3f, the fluorescence intensity of the TNTs shows almost no enhancement compared to Ti plate while it was enhanced slightly for TNTs/CDs and TNTs/Au, indicating their poor photocatalytic performance under 808 nm NIR light irradiation. In contrast, the fluorescence intensity of the TNTs/Au/CDs has an obvious enhancement after 15 min irradiation by 808 nm NIR light, indicating that the prepared surface system of TNTs/Au/CDs has an enhanced photocatalytic property under 808 nm NIR light.

The above results reveal that the TNTs/Au/CDs has the best photocatalytic properties among these five kinds of samples due to the higher separation efficiency of electron and hole in TNTs/Au/CDs composite under 808 nm NIR light irradiation, which is different traditional UV or visible light photocatalytic materials [35,36]. As illustrated in Fig. 3g, when 808 nm NIR light is transformed into the visible light by upconversion of CDs, the excited hot electrons with high energy on the Au NPs can overcome the Schottky barriers and transfer to the conduction band of TNTs to form an electron–hole in composite [37], thus TNTs can be regarded as an electrics receiver to increase the separation efficiency of electron and hole in the system [38].

Bacteria can be killed not only by ROS, but also by hyperthermia. Since the exorbitant temperature will cause tissue burns [39], a suitable thermal effect will assist ROS to kill bacteria without damaging normal tissues [40]. The photothermal heating curves and the thermal image of the samples are shown in Fig. 4a and b, respectively. Both pure Ti and the TNTs samples show poor photothermal property, i.e., after 15 min 808 nm NIR light irradiation, the surface temperature can only increase to 40 °C and 43.1 °C, respectively. According to the photothermal image, the TNTs/CDs, TNTs/Au and TNTs/Au/CDs have distinct photothermal property with a photothermal temperature of 47.6, 50, and 52.5 °C after 15 min 808 nm light irradiation, respectively, due to the intrinsic photothermal properties of CDs and Au NPs. The best photothermal effects of TNTs/Au/CDs should be ascribed to the combination of the CDs and Au NPs with the enhancement for SPR effect of Au NPs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Photocatalytic and photothermal disinfection under 808 nm light irradiation
ce:section.ce:para: As shown in Fig. S5a and b, the spread plate images show that there are a number of bacterial colonies in the Ti groups (control group) in either 15 min 808 nm NIR light or dark surroundings, indicating that 15 min light irradiation has no influence on the viability of bacteria. Compared to the control group, the number of bacteria (both S. aureus and E. coli) on the plates of TNTs are almost the same with the control group, which means that the TNTs have no antibacterial activities with or without light, because the TNTs has no photodynamic and photothermal properties under 808 nm NIR light. As shown in Fig. 5a, the TNTs/CDs and TNTs/Au groups display insufficient antibacterial properties toward S. aureus (28.81 ± 3.1% and 38.87 ± 5.2%, respectively) and E. coli colonies (45.21 ± 4.3% and 56.35 ± 6.2%, respectively), which is ascribed to the poor photothermal effect alone in these groups. In contrast, the TNTs/Au/CDs sample exhibits superior antibacterial properties after 15 min 808 nm NIR light irradiation, there are only few bacterial colonies on the plates (Fig. S5a and b). The corresponding antibacterial efficiency is 96.19 ± 2.3% and 99.89 ± 0.1% against S. aureus and E. coli (Fig. 5a), respectively, suggesting that the TNTs/Au/CDs can kill the bacteria rapidly under 20 min 808 nm NIR light irradiation. Comparing with recent report (94.46% against E. coli in 24 h), our system has better antibacterial efficiency in less time [41,42]. To further investigate the antibacterial properties on the surface of samples. The Live and dead bacterial fluorescence was shown in Fig. 5c and d. The live bacteria can emit green fluorescence while the dead bacteria can emit red fluorescence. From the fluorescence images and the corresponding fluorescence statistics evaluation (calculated by Image J software) (Fig. S5c and d), the bacterial fluorescence in the TNTs/Au/CDs group is almost red, which means that the TNTs/Au/CDs sample has the most effective antibacterial efficiency under 15 min 808 nm NIR light irradiation. This result is consistent with the previous spread plate results shown in Fig. S5a and b. In order to exclude the influence of temperature, the antibacterial tests in ice-bath are shown in Fig. S6, the TNTs/Au/CDs still has 49.49 ± 5.6% and 59.48 ± 6.4% antibacterial efficiency against S. aureus and E. coli in ice-bath under 808 nm NIR because of the single ROS produced.

As shown in Fig. 6a and b, both S. aureus and E. coli cultured with the control and TNTs groups exhibit normal and intact morphologies despite of the conditions, indicating that these two groups have no antibacterial properties with or without light irradiation. In the dark condition, the bacteria also keep intact on the surface of TNTs/Au/CDs, suggesting that this sample almost has no antibacterial activity in the dark. As a contrast, after 15 min 808 nm NIR light irradiation, both S. aureus and E. coli become distorted on the surface of TNTs/Au/CDs (marked with red arrows in Fig. 6a and b). The above phenomena indicate that the NIR light can inspire TNTs/Au/CDs to kill bacteria by making their membranes broken within a short time. When bacterial membranes are damaged, the protein will leak out. As shown in Fig. 6c and d, the leakage level of proteins from bacteria on TNTs/CDs and TNTs/Au groups is about 1.2–1.5 times than on the control group after 15 min 808 nm NIR light irradiation, indicating the slight damage of bacterial membrane on the two groups due to their weak bacteria-killing efficacy under NIR irradiation, which is consistent with spread plate results in Fig. S4. In contrast, the TNTs/Au/CDs group has the maximum protein leakage which is about 2 and 2.2 times than the control group with S. aureus and E. coli, respectively, indicating that the TNTs/Au/CDs can effectively kill bacteria by greatly damaging their membranes under 15 min 808 nm NIR irradiation. ATP is an essential energy element in bacteria, which can maintain the metabolism of the membrane. If the ATP level decreases into the abnormal level, the membrane cannot work normally and become wrinkled. As shown in Fig. 6e and f, the ATP level almost has no change in the dark for all groups. However, after NIR light irradiation, the ATP level of both S. aureus and E. coli on the surface of TNTs/Au/CDs decreases significantly due to the photoinspired ROS and moderate heat that are originated from the enhanced NIR photocatalytic property and photothermal effect, which is schematically illustrated in Fig. 7 .

The morphologies of the cells observed by fluorescence staining can reflect the cytotoxicity and differentiation trend of samples [43]. As shown in Fig. 8a, the cells on the samples are dyed with the FITC and DAPI after culturing for 24 h. All cells display a spindle and spreading morphology. The number and the area of the cells (Fig. S7a and b) in all groups are similar to the control group, suggesting that all samples have no obvious cytotoxicity [43]. The cell aspect ratio (Fig. S6c) of the TNTs/Au and TNTs/Au/CDs show lesser increase than other groups, indicating that the cells on the two groups may exhibit the osteogenic differential tendency after 1 day culturing [43]. The cell viability of the samples was measured by MTT assay (Fig. 8b). As shown in Fig. 8b, all samples do not exhibit obvious cytotoxicity compared to Ti after culturing for 1, 3 and 7 days, suggesting the excellent biocompatibility of TNTs, CDs and Au NPs for cells, which shows better biocompatibility than others previously reported materials (the cells viability is less than 80% with Hepatocellular) [44]. The ALP activities of the samples were measured after culturing for 3, 7 and 14 days (Fig. 8c) to analyze the osteogenic properties of the samples [39]. There is no obvious difference in all groups after culturing for 3 days, indicating that the differentiation in these groups does not happen in 3 days. After culturing for 7 and 14 days, all groups exhibit enhanced ALP activity compared to 3 days, especially, both TNTs/Au and TNTs/Au/CDs show much higher ALP activity than other groups, indicating that the Au NPs in the system can promote osteogenesis [45]. These results reveal that the prepared TNTs/Au/CDs surface system is not only biocompatible but also can enhance the osteogenesis of Ti implants.

In summary, we have exhibited a novel ternary system TNTs/Au/CDs with enhanced NIR photocatalysis and photothermal effects simultaneously. The CDs in this system can convert 808 nm NIR light into the visible light to inspire TNTs to generate ROS. Au NPs can not only catalyze TNTs to strengthen the photocatalytic property of TNTs, but also be triggered to produce photothermal effects due to the surface plasmon resonance (SPR) effect. The enhanced photocatalysis and local photothermy can endow the metallic implants with a highly effective antibacterial efficacy within a very short time due to the synergistic effect of ROS and moderate hyperthermia, which can intensively destroy the bacterial membrane and thus induce the leakage of proteins in the bacteria. Under 808 nm NIR light irradiation for 15 min, the antibacterial efficacy of TNTs/Au/CDs against Staphylococcus aureus and Escherichia coli can reach up to 96.19% and 99.89%, respectively. In addition, The TNTs/Au/CDs system is not only biocompatible but also can promote osteogenic differentiation of MC3T3-E1 cells. Therefore, in consideration of the facile synthetic route, enhance NIR photocatalytic activity, rapid and highly effective disinfection, and great biocompatibility, this surface system can be promising for applications in public health and medical disinfection.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was jointly supported by National Key Research and Development Program of China No. 2016YFC1100600 (sub-project 2016YFC1100604), the Natural Science Foundation of Hubei Province No. 2018CFA064, National Natural Science Foundation of China (Nos. 51671081, 51871162, and 51801056), Hong Kong Research Grants Council (RGC) General Research Funds (GRF) Nos. 11301215, 11205617 and 17214516, and RGC/NSFC (N_HKU725-16), Hong Kong ITC (ITS/287/17, GHX/002/14SZ), as well as Health and Medical Research Fund (No. 03142446).
